Design an Inventory Management System
MEDIUM
role
Senior Software Engineer (SDE-3)
Round 3
Low-Level Design Round
Low-Level Design (LLD)
exp
#714 · Jun 2026
Low-Level Design (LLD)
Object-Oriented Design
Concurrency
Two-Phase Reservation
State Machine
Design Patterns

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design an inventory management system. Stock for each SKU (stock-keeping unit) is split into available and reserved (held) units, and a two-phase reserve -> confirm/release flow protects against overselling while a customer is checking out. Reserving sets stock aside and returns a hold; confirming turns the hold into a sale; releasing gives it back.

Core Requirements:

Add Stock: add_stock(sku, quantity) registers a SKU and seeds/restocks its available units.
Reserve (phase 1): reserve(sku_id, quantity) -> reservation_id moves units available -> reserved and returns a hold id; rejects insufficient stock.
Confirm (phase 2a): confirm(reservation_id) moves the held units reserved -> sold; a hold can be confirmed exactly once.
Release (phase 2b): release(reservation_id) moves held units reserved -> available; idempotent (safe to call twice).
Query: available(sku_id) and a full available/reserved/sold breakdown.
Optional hold expiry: a pluggable policy times out abandoned holds; a background sweep reclaims them.

Additional Considerations:

No Oversell: concurrent reserves on a limited-stock SKU must never exceed available stock. available + reserved + sold is an exact conservation invariant.
Per-SKU locking so different SKUs do not contend.
Thread-safety under concurrent reserve / confirm / release.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. InventoryService, StockItem, Reservation, ReservationExpiryPolicy).
Model the Reservation as a state machine (HELD -> CONFIRMED / RELEASED / EXPIRED).
Discuss design patterns (Two-Phase Reserve/Commit, State, Strategy, Template Method, Lock Striping).
Focus on extensibility for new expiry policies and a clean concurrency model.
Let me build and verify the design before presenting it.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Inventory Management System.

The "nouns" of the system live here: the status of a reservation, the typed
errors callers can catch, and the two entities that own state — `StockItem`
(the per-SKU counts: available / reserved / sold) and `Reservation` (one hold
moving through its own little life: HELD -> CONFIRMED / RELEASED / EXPIRED).

🎙️ "The whole trick is splitting responsibility: the StockItem guards the
COUNTS so they can never go negative or lose a unit, and the Reservation guards
its own LIFECYCLE so a hold can't be confirmed twice. The service just wires the
two together under a per-SKU lock."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


# ── VOCABULARY ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class ReservationStatus(Enum):
    # WHY: a hold is a tiny state machine. Naming the states here means every
    #      other file shares one vocabulary instead of passing raw strings.
    HELD = "HELD"            # stock is set aside but not yet paid for
    CONFIRMED = "CONFIRMED"  # the sale went through; the units are sold
    RELEASED = "RELEASED"    # the hold was let go; units went back to available
    EXPIRED = "EXPIRED"      # the hold timed out before confirm; auto-released


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class InventoryError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises — callers catch one type."""


class SkuNotFoundError(InventoryError):
    """Operation referenced a SKU that was never stocked."""


class ReservationNotFoundError(InventoryError):
    """confirm()/release() referenced a reservation id we never issued."""


class InvalidQuantityError(InventoryError):
    """A quantity was zero or negative — a request must move at least one unit."""


class InsufficientStockError(InventoryError):
    """Not enough AVAILABLE units to satisfy a reserve — this is the oversell guard."""


class InvalidReservationStateError(InventoryError):
    """A lifecycle transition is illegal — e.g. confirming an already-confirmed hold."""


# ── VALUE OBJECT: a read-only snapshot of one SKU's counts ───────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class StockSnapshot:
    """Immutable view returned to callers who want the full breakdown at once.

    🎙️ "I hand back a frozen snapshot, not the live StockItem, so a caller can
    read the numbers but can never reach in and corrupt my counts."
    """
    sku_id: str
    available: int
    reserved: int
    sold: int

    @property
    def total(self) -> int:
        # WHY: this is the conservation number — it must never change except
        #      when new stock is added. The race test asserts exactly this.
        return self.available + self.reserved + self.sold


# ── ENTITY: the SKU identity (never changes once created) ────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Sku:
    """A stock-keeping unit: a stable id plus a human-readable name.

    Frozen on purpose — a SKU's identity is fixed; only its COUNTS move, and
    those live on StockItem, not here.
    """
    sku_id: str
    name: str


# ── ENTITY: the per-SKU counts (the thing the invariant lives on) ────────────
@dataclass
class StockItem:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Owns the three buckets every unit of a SKU is always in EXACTLY one of:
    # available (free to sell), reserved (held by a pending checkout), sold
    # (confirmed). Every method moves units BETWEEN buckets, so the total is
    # conserved by construction — you literally cannot write a method that
    # creates or destroys a unit.
    # 🎙️ "available + reserved + sold is constant. Reserve moves available->
    #      reserved, confirm moves reserved->sold, release moves reserved->
    #      available. Nothing else touches the counts."
    sku_id: str
    available: int
    reserved: int = 0
    sold: int = 0

    def reserve(self, quantity: int) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the oversell guard. We refuse the move BEFORE touching
        #      any count, so a rejected reserve leaves the buckets untouched.
        if quantity > self.available:
            raise InsufficientStockError(
                f"sku '{self.sku_id}': need {quantity}, only {self.available} available"
            )
        self.available -= quantity
        self.reserved += quantity

    def confirm(self, quantity: int) -> None:
        # WHY: you can only confirm units that are currently held. Guarding
        #      against reserved underflow means a double-confirm can never
        #      silently push 'sold' past what was actually held.
        if quantity > self.reserved:
            raise InvalidReservationStateError(
                f"sku '{self.sku_id}': cannot confirm {quantity}, only {self.reserved} reserved"
            )
        self.reserved -= quantity
        self.sold += quantity

    def release(self, quantity: int) -> None:
        # WHY: releasing returns held units to the available pool. Same
        #      underflow guard so we never restore more than we held.
        if quantity > self.reserved:
            raise InvalidReservationStateError(
                f"sku '{self.sku_id}': cannot release {quantity}, only {self.reserved} reserved"
            )
        self.reserved -= quantity
        self.available += quantity

    def add(self, quantity: int) -> None:
        # WHY: the only operation that grows the total — restocking. Everything
        #      else just shuffles units between the three buckets.
        self.available += quantity

    @property
    def total(self) -> int:
        return self.available + self.reserved + self.sold

    def snapshot(self) -> StockSnapshot:
        return StockSnapshot(self.sku_id, self.available, self.reserved, self.sold)


# ── ENTITY: one reservation's lifecycle (the state machine) ──────────────────
@dataclass
class Reservation:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A single hold. It owns ONE thing the StockItem must not: its lifecycle.
    # The legal moves are HELD -> CONFIRMED, HELD -> RELEASED, HELD -> EXPIRED.
    # Because only this class flips the status, an illegal jump (confirm twice,
    # release after confirm) is impossible by construction — the service never
    # writes `reservation.status = ...` directly.
    id: str
    sku_id: str
    quantity: int
    status: ReservationStatus
    created_at: float
    expires_at: float | None

    def confirm(self) -> None:
        # WHY: a hold can be confirmed exactly once. The second confirm hits
        #      this guard and is rejected — that's the "double-confirm rejected"
        #      requirement enforced at the entity, not scattered in the service.
        if self.status is not ReservationStatus.HELD:
            raise InvalidReservationStateError(
                f"reservation '{self.id}' is {self.status.value}; cannot confirm"
            )
        self.status = ReservationStatus.CONFIRMED

    def release(self) -> bool:
        # WHY: release is safe to call twice — the second call does nothing and
        #      returns False, so a user-cancel racing the expiry sweep can't
        #      double-restore stock. A CONFIRMED hold, though, is a real sale
        #      and must NOT be silently released, so that path raises.
        if self.status in (ReservationStatus.RELEASED, ReservationStatus.EXPIRED):
            return False
        if self.status is ReservationStatus.CONFIRMED:
            raise InvalidReservationStateError(
                f"reservation '{self.id}' is CONFIRMED; a confirmed sale cannot be released"
            )
        self.status = ReservationStatus.RELEASED
        return True

    def expire(self) -> bool:
        # WHY: only a still-HELD hold can expire. Returns False if it was
        #      already confirmed/released so the sweep skips it cleanly.
        if self.status is not ReservationStatus.HELD:
            return False
        self.status = ReservationStatus.EXPIRED
        return True

    def is_expired(self, now: float) -> bool:
        # WHY: a hold with no expiry (expires_at is None) never times out.
        return self.expires_at is not None and now >= self.expires_at

# Python 3.11+
"""Reservation expiry policy — the seam that decides WHEN a hold should time out.

🎙️ "The service doesn't hardcode '15-minute holds'. It asks a policy: given the
moment a hold was created, when does it expire? Cart checkouts might want 15
minutes, a flash-sale 90 seconds, an internal transfer never. Swapping that rule
is a new class — the service never changes."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod


class ReservationExpiryPolicy(ABC):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The abstraction the service depends on (Dependency Inversion). It answers
    # one question: "for a hold created at time T, what is its expiry instant?"
    # Returning None means "this hold never expires".
    @abstractmethod
    def expires_at(self, created_at: float) -> float | None:
        """Return the absolute time the hold expires, or None for no expiry."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class NoExpiryPolicy(ReservationExpiryPolicy):
    """Holds live until explicitly confirmed or released. (Default.)"""

    def expires_at(self, created_at: float) -> float | None:
        # WHY: None is the signal Reservation.is_expired() reads to mean
        #      "never times out" — so the sweep simply never reclaims it.
        return None


class FixedTtlExpiryPolicy(ReservationExpiryPolicy):
    """Every hold expires a fixed number of seconds after it was created.

    🎙️ "This is the cart-timeout policy — a hold you don't pay for in N seconds
    is auto-released so the stock isn't stranded."
    """

    def __init__(self, ttl_seconds: float) -> None:
        # WHY: a non-positive TTL would expire holds instantly (or in the past),
        #      which is never what a caller means — reject it at construction.
        if ttl_seconds <= 0:
            raise ValueError("ttl_seconds must be positive")
        self._ttl = ttl_seconds

    def expires_at(self, created_at: float) -> float | None:
        return created_at + self._ttl

# Python 3.11+
"""InventoryService — the orchestrator — plus its thread-safe subclass.

The clean core below has ZERO lock code: `_lock(sku_id)` is a no-op hook. The
two-phase flow lives here: `reserve` sets stock aside and hands back a hold id,
then `confirm` turns the hold into a sale or `release` gives it back. The
`ThreadSafeInventoryService` subclass at the bottom overrides the one hook with
a per-SKU reentrant lock (lock striping) so two different SKUs never contend.

🎙️ "Reserve, confirm, release. The service coordinates; the StockItem owns the
counts and the Reservation owns its lifecycle. The single concurrency risk is
the check-then-act inside reserve — I keep the core clean and fix it in the
thread-safe subclass with one lock per SKU."
"""

import itertools
import threading
import time
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    InsufficientStockError,
    InvalidQuantityError,
    InvalidReservationStateError,
    Reservation,
    ReservationNotFoundError,
    ReservationStatus,
    Sku,
    SkuNotFoundError,
    StockItem,
    StockSnapshot,
)
from strategies import NoExpiryPolicy, ReservationExpiryPolicy


class InventoryService:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The public API and the single source of truth. It owns two registries:
    # SKU id -> StockItem (the counts) and reservation id -> Reservation (the
    # in-flight holds it can confirm, release, or sweep). It never duplicates
    # the counts or the lifecycle logic — it asks the entities.
    def __init__(
        self,
        expiry_policy: ReservationExpiryPolicy | None = None,
        now_fn=time.monotonic,
    ) -> None:
        # WHY: time is injected as a function so tests can fast-forward the
        #      clock instead of sleeping — expiry tests run in microseconds.
        self._now = now_fn
        self._policy = expiry_policy or NoExpiryPolicy()
        self._stock: dict[str, StockItem] = {}
        self._skus: dict[str, Sku] = {}
        self._reservations: dict[str, Reservation] = {}
        # WHY: itertools.count.__next__ is atomic in CPython, so two threads
        #      reserving different SKUs still get unique ids with no extra lock.
        self._ids = itertools.count(1)

    # ── the swap-in concurrency hook (Template Method) ──
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, sku_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does nothing in the clean core — there are no locks in this file's
        #      logic. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method. The
        #      diff between "clean" and "safe" is one method, not a rewrite.
        yield

    # ── add_stock: register a SKU and grow its available pool ──
    def add_stock(self, sku: Sku, quantity: int) -> None:
        """Stock (or restock) a SKU by `quantity` units of available inventory."""
        self._require_positive(quantity)
        with self._lock(sku.sku_id):
            item = self._stock.get(sku.sku_id)
            if item is None:
                self._skus[sku.sku_id] = sku
                self._stock[sku.sku_id] = StockItem(sku_id=sku.sku_id, available=quantity)
                return
            item.add(quantity)

    # ── reserve: PHASE 1 — set stock aside, return a hold id ──
    def reserve(self, sku_id: str, quantity: int) -> str:
        """Hold `quantity` units of `sku_id`. Returns the reservation id.

        Raises InvalidQuantityError, SkuNotFoundError, InsufficientStockError.
        """
        self._require_positive(quantity)
        item = self._require_stock(sku_id)
        with self._lock(sku_id):
            # WHY: check-then-act — item.reserve() both CHECKS availability and
            #      DECREMENTS it. Under the per-SKU lock these two are one
            #      atomic step, so two racers can't both see the last unit.
            item.reserve(quantity)            # raises InsufficientStockError if oversold
            created = self._now()
            res = Reservation(
                id=self._next_id(),
                sku_id=sku_id,
                quantity=quantity,
                status=ReservationStatus.HELD,
                created_at=created,
                expires_at=self._policy.expires_at(created),
            )
            self._reservations[res.id] = res
            return res.id

    # ── confirm: PHASE 2a — turn a hold into a sale ──
    def confirm(self, reservation_id: str) -> None:
        """Confirm a held reservation: reserved units become sold.

        Raises ReservationNotFoundError, InvalidReservationStateError
        (already confirmed/released, or the hold has expired).
        """
        res = self._require_reservation(reservation_id)
        with self._lock(res.sku_id):
            item = self._stock[res.sku_id]
            self._expire_if_due(res, item)    # lazily reclaim an expired hold first
            res.confirm()                     # rejects double-confirm / expired
            item.confirm(res.quantity)

    # ── release: PHASE 2b — give a hold back ──
    def release(self, reservation_id: str) -> None:
        """Release a held reservation: reserved units return to available.

        Idempotent — releasing an already-released/expired hold is a no-op.
        Raises ReservationNotFoundError, InvalidReservationStateError
        (the hold was already confirmed — a sale cannot be un-sold here).
        """
        res = self._require_reservation(reservation_id)
        with self._lock(res.sku_id):
            item = self._stock[res.sku_id]
            if self._expire_if_due(res, item):
                return                        # the sweep path already restored stock
            if res.release():                 # False => already terminal, no-op
                item.release(res.quantity)

    # ── available: how many units are free to sell right now ──
    def available(self, sku_id: str) -> int:
        with self._lock(sku_id):
            return self._require_stock(sku_id).available

    # ── snapshot: the full available/reserved/sold breakdown ──
    def snapshot(self, sku_id: str) -> StockSnapshot:
        with self._lock(sku_id):
            return self._require_stock(sku_id).snapshot()

    # ── sweep_expired: background cleanup of timed-out holds ──
    def sweep_expired(self) -> list[str]:
        """Release every hold whose timer has elapsed; return the reclaimed ids.

        A background timer thread calls this on an interval. Idempotent: each
        already-terminal hold is skipped, so calling it twice reclaims nothing.
        """
        now = self._now()
        reclaimed: list[str] = []
        for res in list(self._reservations.values()):
            if res.status is not ReservationStatus.HELD or not res.is_expired(now):
                continue
            with self._lock(res.sku_id):
                item = self._stock[res.sku_id]
                if self._expire_if_due(res, item):
                    reclaimed.append(res.id)
        return reclaimed

    # ── private helpers ──
    def _expire_if_due(self, res: Reservation, item: StockItem) -> bool:
        # WHY: one place decides "this hold timed out" and does the two coupled
        #      moves together — flip the reservation to EXPIRED and hand its
        #      units back to available. Returns True if it acted.
        if res.status is ReservationStatus.HELD and res.is_expired(self._now()):
            if res.expire():
                item.release(res.quantity)
                return True
        return False

    def _next_id(self) -> str:
        return f"res-{next(self._ids)}"

    @staticmethod
    def _require_positive(quantity: int) -> None:
        if quantity <= 0:
            raise InvalidQuantityError(f"quantity must be positive, got {quantity}")

    def _require_stock(self, sku_id: str) -> StockItem:
        item = self._stock.get(sku_id)
        if item is None:
            raise SkuNotFoundError(f"unknown sku '{sku_id}'")
        return item

    def _require_reservation(self, reservation_id: str) -> Reservation:
        res = self._reservations.get(reservation_id)
        if res is None:
            raise ReservationNotFoundError(f"unknown reservation '{reservation_id}'")
        return res


class ThreadSafeInventoryService(InventoryService):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The ONLY change from the clean core is the `_lock` hook. We keep one
    # RLock per SKU (lock striping) so callers touching DIFFERENT SKUs never
    # wait on each other — only same-SKU calls serialize, which is exactly the
    # contention we must serialize to prevent oversell.
    def __init__(
        self,
        expiry_policy: ReservationExpiryPolicy | None = None,
        now_fn=time.monotonic,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__(expiry_policy=expiry_policy, now_fn=now_fn)
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()     # guards the dict of per-SKU locks

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, sku_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: lock the MAP first to fetch-or-create this SKU's lock safely —
        #      two threads adding the same key at once could otherwise corrupt
        #      the dict. Then hold the per-SKU RLock for the critical section.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._locks.get(sku_id)
            if lock is None:
                lock = threading.RLock()       # reentrant: a guarded method may call another
                self._locks[sku_id] = lock
        with lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Inventory Management System.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
Cases cover the two-phase flow (reserve -> confirm, reserve -> release), the
double-confirm guard, a contended oversell attempt that is cleanly rejected,
and an expiring hold reclaimed by the sweep.
"""

import threading

from inventory_service import InventoryService, ThreadSafeInventoryService
from models import (
    InsufficientStockError,
    InvalidReservationStateError,
    ReservationStatus,
    Sku,
)
from strategies import FixedTtlExpiryPolicy


def main() -> None:
    # ── Case 1: reserve -> confirm reduces sellable stock ──
    svc = InventoryService()
    widget = Sku("WIDGET", "Blue Widget")
    svc.add_stock(widget, 10)
    res_id = svc.reserve("WIDGET", 3)
    snap_held = svc.snapshot("WIDGET")
    svc.confirm(res_id)
    snap_sold = svc.snapshot("WIDGET")
    print(
        f"Case 1: reserve(3) -> held(avail={snap_held.available}, res={snap_held.reserved}) "
        f"| confirm -> sold(avail={snap_sold.available}, sold={snap_sold.sold})"
    )
    assert (snap_held.available, snap_held.reserved) == (7, 3)
    assert (snap_sold.available, snap_sold.reserved, snap_sold.sold) == (7, 0, 3)
    assert snap_sold.total == 10
    print("Case 1: a confirmed reservation moves units from available to sold -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: reserve -> release restores stock ──
    rid = svc.reserve("WIDGET", 4)
    assert svc.available("WIDGET") == 3            # 7 free - 4 held
    svc.release(rid)
    after = svc.snapshot("WIDGET")
    print(f"Case 2: reserve(4) then release -> avail back to {after.available}, reserved={after.reserved}")
    assert (after.available, after.reserved, after.sold) == (7, 0, 3)
    svc.release(rid)                                # idempotent: second release is a no-op
    assert svc.available("WIDGET") == 7
    print("Case 2: releasing a hold returns its units; double-release is a safe no-op -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: double-confirm is rejected ──
    rid2 = svc.reserve("WIDGET", 2)
    svc.confirm(rid2)
    try:
        svc.confirm(rid2)
        assert False, "second confirm should have raised"
    except InvalidReservationStateError as e:
        print(f"Case 3: second confirm rejected -> {e}")
    print("Case 3: a reservation can be confirmed exactly once -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: contended oversell — all threads race at one instant ──
    safe = ThreadSafeInventoryService()
    safe.add_stock(Sku("FLASH", "Flash Sale Item"), 5)   # only 5 units exist
    n_threads = 40
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
    wins: list[str] = []
    losses: list[InsufficientStockError] = []
    wins_lock = threading.Lock()

    def grab() -> None:
        barrier.wait()                              # everyone hits reserve() together
        try:
            rid_local = safe.reserve("FLASH", 1)
            with wins_lock:
                wins.append(rid_local)
        except InsufficientStockError as exc:
            with wins_lock:
                losses.append(exc)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=grab) for _ in range(n_threads)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    snap = safe.snapshot("FLASH")
    print(
        f"Case 4: {n_threads} threads raced for 5 units -> {len(wins)} reserved, "
        f"{len(losses)} rejected | avail={snap.available} reserved={snap.reserved} total={snap.total}"
    )
    assert len(wins) == 5                           # exactly the stock, never more
    assert len(set(wins)) == 5                      # every winner has a unique hold id
    assert len(losses) == n_threads - 5             # everyone else rejected cleanly
    assert snap.reserved == 5 and snap.available == 0
    assert snap.total == 5                          # conservation: nothing created or lost
    print("Case 4: under a 40-thread race exactly 5 units reserve, no oversell -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: an unconfirmed hold expires and the sweep reclaims it ──
    clock = {"t": 1000.0}
    timed = InventoryService(expiry_policy=FixedTtlExpiryPolicy(60), now_fn=lambda: clock["t"])
    timed.add_stock(Sku("CART", "Cart Item"), 8)
    hold = timed.reserve("CART", 3)
    assert timed.available("CART") == 5
    clock["t"] = 1000.0 + 61                         # jump past the 60s TTL — no real sleep
    reclaimed = timed.sweep_expired()
    snap_c = timed.snapshot("CART")
    print(f"Case 5: hold {hold} expired after TTL -> sweep reclaimed {reclaimed}, avail back to {snap_c.available}")
    assert reclaimed == [hold]
    assert (snap_c.available, snap_c.reserved) == (8, 0)
    try:
        timed.confirm(hold)
        assert False, "confirming an expired hold should raise"
    except InvalidReservationStateError:
        pass
    assert timed._reservations[hold].status is ReservationStatus.EXPIRED
    print("Case 5: a timed-out hold is auto-released by the sweep; confirm then fails -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Inventory Management System.

Covers: the StockItem count machine and the Reservation lifecycle (unit), every
public service method and its typed error path, idempotent release, hold expiry
with an injected clock, and THE concurrency proof — N threads reserve from a
limited SKU through a threading.Barrier, asserting EXACT conservation
(available + reserved + sold is invariant) with no oversell.
"""

import threading

import pytest

from inventory_service import InventoryService, ThreadSafeInventoryService
from models import (
    InsufficientStockError,
    InvalidQuantityError,
    InvalidReservationStateError,
    Reservation,
    ReservationNotFoundError,
    ReservationStatus,
    Sku,
    SkuNotFoundError,
    StockItem,
)
from strategies import FixedTtlExpiryPolicy, NoExpiryPolicy


# ── fixtures ──
@pytest.fixture
def clock() -> dict:
    return {"t": 1000.0}


@pytest.fixture
def svc() -> InventoryService:
    s = InventoryService()
    s.add_stock(Sku("SKU1", "Item One"), 10)
    return s


# ── StockItem (unit): the count machine conserves units ──
def test_stockitem_reserve_moves_available_to_reserved():
    item = StockItem(sku_id="X", available=10)
    item.reserve(4)
    assert (item.available, item.reserved, item.sold) == (6, 4, 0)
    assert item.total == 10


def test_stockitem_reserve_beyond_available_raises():
    item = StockItem(sku_id="X", available=3)
    with pytest.raises(InsufficientStockError):
        item.reserve(4)
    assert (item.available, item.reserved) == (3, 0)   # untouched on rejection


def test_stockitem_confirm_then_total_conserved():
    item = StockItem(sku_id="X", available=10)
    item.reserve(4)
    item.confirm(4)
    assert (item.available, item.reserved, item.sold) == (6, 0, 4)
    assert item.total == 10


def test_stockitem_release_restores_available():
    item = StockItem(sku_id="X", available=10)
    item.reserve(4)
    item.release(4)
    assert (item.available, item.reserved, item.sold) == (10, 0, 0)


# ── Reservation (unit): the lifecycle state machine ──
def test_reservation_confirm_once_then_rejects():
    r = Reservation("res-1", "X", 2, ReservationStatus.HELD, created_at=0.0, expires_at=None)
    r.confirm()
    assert r.status is ReservationStatus.CONFIRMED
    with pytest.raises(InvalidReservationStateError):
        r.confirm()


def test_reservation_release_is_idempotent():
    r = Reservation("res-1", "X", 2, ReservationStatus.HELD, created_at=0.0, expires_at=None)
    assert r.release() is True
    assert r.release() is False                       # second call is a no-op
    assert r.status is ReservationStatus.RELEASED


def test_reservation_confirmed_cannot_be_released():
    r = Reservation("res-1", "X", 2, ReservationStatus.HELD, created_at=0.0, expires_at=None)
    r.confirm()
    with pytest.raises(InvalidReservationStateError):
        r.release()


def test_reservation_is_expired_respects_none():
    never = Reservation("res-1", "X", 1, ReservationStatus.HELD, created_at=0.0, expires_at=None)
    timed = Reservation("res-2", "X", 1, ReservationStatus.HELD, created_at=0.0, expires_at=50.0)
    assert never.is_expired(10_000) is False
    assert timed.is_expired(49.9) is False
    assert timed.is_expired(50.0) is True


# ── service happy paths ──
def test_reserve_then_confirm_reduces_stock(svc):
    rid = svc.reserve("SKU1", 3)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 7
    svc.confirm(rid)
    snap = svc.snapshot("SKU1")
    assert (snap.available, snap.reserved, snap.sold) == (7, 0, 3)
    assert snap.total == 10


def test_reserve_then_release_restores_stock(svc):
    rid = svc.reserve("SKU1", 4)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 6
    svc.release(rid)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 10
    assert svc.snapshot("SKU1").reserved == 0


def test_release_is_idempotent_via_service(svc):
    rid = svc.reserve("SKU1", 4)
    svc.release(rid)
    svc.release(rid)                                  # safe to call twice
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 10


def test_add_stock_restocks_existing_sku(svc):
    svc.add_stock(Sku("SKU1", "Item One"), 5)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 15


# ── typed error paths ──
def test_reserve_unknown_sku_raises(svc):
    with pytest.raises(SkuNotFoundError):
        svc.reserve("NOPE", 1)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("qty", [0, -1, -10])
def test_reserve_non_positive_quantity_raises(svc, qty):
    with pytest.raises(InvalidQuantityError):
        svc.reserve("SKU1", qty)


def test_reserve_insufficient_stock_raises(svc):
    with pytest.raises(InsufficientStockError):
        svc.reserve("SKU1", 11)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 10                # nothing consumed on failure


def test_double_confirm_rejected(svc):
    rid = svc.reserve("SKU1", 2)
    svc.confirm(rid)
    with pytest.raises(InvalidReservationStateError):
        svc.confirm(rid)


def test_confirm_unknown_reservation_raises(svc):
    with pytest.raises(ReservationNotFoundError):
        svc.confirm("res-999")


def test_release_confirmed_reservation_raises(svc):
    rid = svc.reserve("SKU1", 2)
    svc.confirm(rid)
    with pytest.raises(InvalidReservationStateError):
        svc.release(rid)


def test_available_unknown_sku_raises(svc):
    with pytest.raises(SkuNotFoundError):
        svc.available("NOPE")


# ── boundary ──
def test_reserve_exactly_all_units(svc):
    rid = svc.reserve("SKU1", 10)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 0
    with pytest.raises(InsufficientStockError):
        svc.reserve("SKU1", 1)
    svc.release(rid)
    assert svc.available("SKU1") == 10


# ── expiry with an injected clock (no real sleeping) ──
def test_hold_expires_and_sweep_reclaims(clock):
    s = InventoryService(expiry_policy=FixedTtlExpiryPolicy(60), now_fn=lambda: clock["t"])
    s.add_stock(Sku("CART", "Cart Item"), 8)
    rid = s.reserve("CART", 3)
    assert s.available("CART") == 5
    clock["t"] += 61                                  # jump past the TTL
    assert s.sweep_expired() == [rid]
    assert s.available("CART") == 8
    assert s._reservations[rid].status is ReservationStatus.EXPIRED


def test_confirm_after_expiry_raises(clock):
    s = InventoryService(expiry_policy=FixedTtlExpiryPolicy(60), now_fn=lambda: clock["t"])
    s.add_stock(Sku("CART", "Cart Item"), 8)
    rid = s.reserve("CART", 3)
    clock["t"] += 61
    with pytest.raises(InvalidReservationStateError):
        s.confirm(rid)
    assert s.available("CART") == 8                   # the expired hold gave units back


def test_no_expiry_policy_never_times_out(clock):
    s = InventoryService(expiry_policy=NoExpiryPolicy(), now_fn=lambda: clock["t"])
    s.add_stock(Sku("CART", "Cart Item"), 8)
    rid = s.reserve("CART", 3)
    clock["t"] += 10_000_000
    assert s.sweep_expired() == []
    s.confirm(rid)                                    # still confirmable
    assert s.snapshot("CART").sold == 3


# ── THE concurrency proof: exact conservation under a Barrier race ──
def test_barrier_oversell_exact_conservation():
    """N threads each reserve 1 unit from a K-stock SKU at one instant.

    Asserts EXACT conservation: exactly K reserve, each with a unique hold id,
    the rest fail with InsufficientStockError, and available + reserved + sold
    equals the original stock (no unit created or lost).
    """
    n_threads = 64
    capacity = 7
    svc = ThreadSafeInventoryService()
    svc.add_stock(Sku("LTD", "Limited Item"), capacity)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
    wins: list[str] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()                                # all N hit reserve() together
        try:
            rid = svc.reserve("LTD", 1)
            with guard:
                wins.append(rid)
        except InsufficientStockError as exc:
            with guard:
                losses.append(exc)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(n_threads)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    snap = svc.snapshot("LTD")
    assert len(wins) == capacity                      # exactly the stock reserved
    assert len(set(wins)) == capacity                 # every winner is a unique hold
    assert len(losses) == n_threads - capacity        # everyone else rejected
    assert all(isinstance(e, InsufficientStockError) for e in losses)
    assert (snap.available, snap.reserved, snap.sold) == (0, capacity, 0)
    assert snap.total == capacity                     # conservation invariant holds


def test_barrier_mixed_reserve_confirm_release_conserves():
    """Concurrent reserve+confirm on one SKU and reserve+release on another:
    totals are conserved on both SKUs regardless of interleaving."""
    svc = ThreadSafeInventoryService()
    svc.add_stock(Sku("A", "Item A"), 50)
    svc.add_stock(Sku("B", "Item B"), 50)
    n = 50
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def buy_a() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        rid = svc.reserve("A", 1)
        svc.confirm(rid)

    def churn_b() -> None:
        rid = svc.reserve("B", 1)
        svc.release(rid)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=buy_a) for _ in range(n)]
    threads += [threading.Thread(target=churn_b) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    a = svc.snapshot("A")
    b = svc.snapshot("B")
    assert (a.available, a.reserved, a.sold) == (0, 0, 50) and a.total == 50
    assert (b.available, b.reserved, b.sold) == (50, 0, 0) and b.total == 50

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate "available vs reserved stock", ask clarifying questions about the two-phase flow. Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — name the 7 public calls (`add_stock`, `reserve`, `confirm`, `release`, `available`, `snapshot`, `sweep_expired`). "Before classes, here's the contract." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — `InventoryService`, `StockItem`, `Reservation`, `ReservationExpiryPolicy`. Mention `Sku`/`StockSnapshot` in one sentence, don't draw them. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Two-phase reserve/confirm, State machine, Strategy. Then: "I see a check-then-act race in `reserve` — I'll fix it with a per-SKU lock at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the insight: split COUNTS (StockItem) from LIFECYCLE (Reservation); reserve is the only race. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (see §6.0): enums → `StockItem` → `Reservation` state machine → `ReservationExpiryPolicy` → `InventoryService` skeleton → `reserve()` → `confirm()`/`release()` | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 4** — reserve→confirm verbally, then the 40-thread oversell race. Mode B: run `main.py`. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-SKU `RLock`, no-op `_lock` hook, lock striping. Write the thread-safe subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — distributed oversell (DB compare-and-set), hold expiry, hot SKU. | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output; trace cases verbally. Write code but do not execute.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show actual terminal output.

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Right after the diagram, say *"there's a check-then-act race in `reserve` — two carts both see the last unit and both succeed, that's an oversell"* — before they ask. That single proactive sentence signals you've built real concurrent inventory systems.

---

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

We're building the stock-counting brain behind an online store's checkout. When a shopper adds the last pair of sneakers to their cart and starts paying, we can't let a second shopper grab that same pair — but we also can't mark it *sold* until the payment actually clears. So the system tracks each product (SKU) in three buckets: **available** (free to sell), **reserved** (held by an in-flight checkout), and **sold** (paid for). The interesting part is the two-step dance — **reserve** a unit while the customer pays, then **confirm** it into a sale or **release** it back if they bail — all while many shoppers hammer the same popular item at once.

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "Let me make sure I've got the core model right — every SKU's stock splits into available, reserved, and sold, and the sum of those three is conserved unless we restock. I'll treat that conservation as the system's central invariant. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes, that's exactly the heart of it."
*✓ locked: available + reserved + sold is the invariant the whole design protects.*

**You:** "I'll model checkout as two phases: `reserve(sku, qty)` sets stock aside and returns a hold id, then `confirm(hold)` turns it into a sale or `release(hold)` gives it back. That keeps 'held during payment' distinct from 'sold'. That the right shape?"
**Interviewer:** "That's the shape we want — payment itself is upstream, you just need reserve/confirm/release."
*✓ scoped: payment gateway is out of scope; we own the stock state machine only.*

**You:** "The make-or-break case is oversell under concurrency: if 40 carts race for the last 5 units, exactly 5 reserve and 35 get a clean error — never 6. I'd guard that with a per-SKU lock so different SKUs don't block each other. Agreed that's the bar?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed — no oversell is the one thing you cannot get wrong here."
*✓ the §9 concurrency proof (Barrier test) is the centerpiece.*

**You:** "I'd assume a hold can optionally expire — a cart you abandon shouldn't strand stock forever. I'll make expiry a pluggable policy (default: never expire) and add a background sweep. Reasonable, or out of scope?"
**Interviewer:** "Keep it — make it optional like you said, that's a nice touch."
*✓ `ReservationExpiryPolicy` + `sweep_expired()` are in; default is no expiry.*

**You:** "Double-confirm and double-release: I'd reject a second `confirm` on the same hold with a typed error, but make `release` idempotent so a user-cancel racing the expiry sweep doesn't double-restore stock. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep — confirm-once, release-safe-twice. Good distinction."
*✓ confirm is strict, release is idempotent — enforced on the `Reservation` entity itself.*

**You:** "I'll treat warehouses/multi-location and the actual product catalog (price, description) as out of scope — we key everything off a `sku_id` string. Fine for this round?"
**Interviewer:** "Fine, single logical pool of stock per SKU."
*✓ one stock pool per SKU; no location sharding (flag for §9 if distributed comes up).*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Add stock for a SKU** by quantity.
   - First call registers the SKU and seeds its available count; later calls restock (add to available).
   - Rejects a non-positive quantity with a typed error.
2. **Reserve units** (phase 1) by `(sku_id, quantity)`.
   - On success moves `quantity` from available → reserved and returns a hold id.
   - Rejects unknown SKU, non-positive quantity, and **insufficient available stock** (the oversell guard) — each with its own typed error.
3. **Confirm a reservation** (phase 2a).
   - Moves the held units from reserved → sold. The hold becomes CONFIRMED.
   - A second confirm on the same hold is rejected; a confirm on an expired hold is rejected.
4. **Release a reservation** (phase 2b).
   - Moves held units back from reserved → available. The hold becomes RELEASED.
   - Idempotent: releasing an already-released/expired hold is a no-op. Releasing a CONFIRMED hold is rejected (a sale can't be un-sold here).
5. **Query stock** — `available(sku_id)` returns free units; `snapshot(sku_id)` returns the full available/reserved/sold breakdown.
6. **Expire stale holds** — an optional policy stamps each hold with an expiry; a `sweep_expired()` releases timed-out holds back to available.
7. **No oversell under concurrency** — concurrent reserves on one SKU never let total reserved+sold exceed the stock ever added.

**Out of scope**
- Payment processing / the checkout UI (upstream — we only move stock state).
- Product catalog data: price, name beyond a display string, images (a separate service).
- Multi-warehouse / geo-sharding (one logical pool per SKU for v1).
- Backorders and waitlists when stock is exhausted (we reject; queueing is a §9 twist).

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK — the one thing I want to lock down before modeling is that available + reserved + sold is a conserved total per SKU, and reserve→confirm/release is the two-phase flow. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."

---

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Checkout service** — calls `reserve` when a cart starts paying, then `confirm` on payment success or `release` on cancel/timeout.
- **Inventory / ops admin** — calls `add_stock` to seed and restock SKUs.
- **Background sweeper** — a timer thread that periodically calls `sweep_expired()` to reclaim abandoned holds.
- **Storefront (read path)** — calls `available`/`snapshot` to show "5 left".

**Core flows**
1. Admin: `add_stock(sku, qty)` → SKU registered → available = qty
2. Checkout (happy): `reserve(sku, qty)` → hold id → customer pays → `confirm(hold)` → units sold
3. Checkout (abandon): `reserve(sku, qty)` → hold id → customer cancels → `release(hold)` → units back to available
4. Sweeper: timer fires → `sweep_expired()` → each timed-out hold → EXPIRED → units back to available
5. Storefront: `available(sku)` → free count shown to shopper

---

## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)

> **SKU (Stock-Keeping Unit)** — The product identity a shopper buys. It has a stable id and a display name, but its identity never changes — only its counts move. *Accepted as a small frozen entity (`Sku`) — but note the COUNTS don't live here.*
>
> **Stock / inventory counts** — The available/reserved/sold numbers for one SKU. This owns real state AND the rules that move units between buckets without ever creating or losing one. That behavior plus state earns it a class. *Accepted as `StockItem` — the entity that guards the conservation invariant.*
>
> **Reservation (hold)** — A pending claim on some units while a checkout is in flight. It has its OWN lifecycle (HELD → CONFIRMED / RELEASED / EXPIRED) and an expiry timestamp — that lifecycle is logic, not just data. *Accepted as `Reservation` — it owns its state machine so illegal transitions are impossible.*
>
> **Quantity** — How many units a request moves. A plain number validated at the boundary; no state, no behavior. *Rejected as entity — it's an input parameter to `reserve`/`confirm`.*
>
> **Order / Cart** — Tempting, but the cart and order live in the checkout service upstream. We only ever see "reserve N of this SKU". *Rejected — out of our system boundary; the hold id is our handle back to the caller's cart.*
>
> **Warehouse / Location** — A real concept, but we scoped to one logical pool per SKU. Adding locations means sharding stock per (sku, location). *Rejected for v1 — a §9 extension, not a v1 entity.*
>
> **AccessToken / hold id** — The string `reserve` returns. It's just the `Reservation.id`; it has no independent lifecycle of its own. *Rejected as a separate entity — it's the id field on `Reservation`.*
>
> **Expiry policy** — The rule deciding WHEN a hold times out. This is behavior the business will want to vary (15-min cart vs 90-sec flash sale vs never), so it factors out cleanly. *Accepted as a Strategy interface `ReservationExpiryPolicy`.*
>
> **Stock snapshot** — The available/reserved/sold breakdown handed to a caller. It's a read-only view, not a thing with behavior. *Accepted as a frozen value object `StockSnapshot` — so callers read numbers but can't mutate my counts.*

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `InventoryService` | Orchestrate the two-phase flow; own the registries | `_stock: dict[str, StockItem]`, `_reservations: dict[str, Reservation]` (its in-flight holds), `_policy: ReservationExpiryPolicy` | Every reserve/confirm/release runs inside the per-SKU critical section |
| `StockItem` | Hold one SKU's counts and move units between buckets | `sku_id: str` (**id**), `available: int`, `reserved: int`, `sold: int` | `available + reserved + sold` is constant except on restock; no bucket goes negative |
| `Reservation` | Own one hold's lifecycle | `id: str` (**id**), `sku_id: str` (which SKU it holds), `quantity: int`, `status: ReservationStatus`, `expires_at: float \| None` | Only legal transitions from HELD; confirm-once; release idempotent |
| `Sku` | Stable product identity | `sku_id: str` (**id**), `name: str` | Frozen — identity never changes |
| `StockSnapshot` | Read-only count view | `sku_id: str`, `available: int`, `reserved: int`, `sold: int` | Frozen — callers can't corrupt live counts |
| `ReservationExpiryPolicy` | Decide a hold's expiry instant | (interface — no state) | Returns a deterministic expiry for a given creation time, or `None` |

**Enums**
- `ReservationStatus`: `HELD`, `CONFIRMED`, `RELEASED`, `EXPIRED`

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro (the natural-language story)

> "OK so the model has about four objects that matter. `InventoryService` is the brain — it owns the map of SKU-id to `StockItem` and the map of reservation-id to `Reservation`, and it coordinates the two-phase flow. `StockItem` is the one that holds the actual numbers — available, reserved, sold — and it's the only thing allowed to move a unit between those buckets, so the total can never silently drift. `Reservation` is the interesting one because it owns its own little life — HELD, then CONFIRMED or RELEASED or EXPIRED — which means a double-confirm is impossible by construction; the service never reaches in and sets the status. Expiry is a pluggable `ReservationExpiryPolicy` so the timeout rule can change without touching the service. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are Cart, Order, Warehouse, and Payment — those live upstream or in a future version; we just key off a `sku_id` and hand back a hold id."

---

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

The orchestrator's public surface — the only methods a Checkout service, admin, or sweeper calls. Everything else (the count moves, the lifecycle flips, the expiry math) is internal.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `add_stock` | `(sku: Sku, quantity: int) -> None` | — | `InvalidQuantityError` | Admin seeds or restocks a SKU's available units |
| `reserve` | `(sku_id: str, quantity: int) -> str` | the hold id | `InvalidQuantityError`, `SkuNotFoundError`, `InsufficientStockError` | Phase 1: set stock aside for an in-flight checkout |
| `confirm` | `(reservation_id: str) -> None` | — | `ReservationNotFoundError`, `InvalidReservationStateError` | Phase 2a: turn a hold into a sale |
| `release` | `(reservation_id: str) -> None` | — | `ReservationNotFoundError`, `InvalidReservationStateError` | Phase 2b: give a hold back to available (idempotent) |
| `available` | `(sku_id: str) -> int` | free units now | `SkuNotFoundError` | Storefront read: how many are sellable |
| `snapshot` | `(sku_id: str) -> StockSnapshot` | available/reserved/sold view | `SkuNotFoundError` | Full breakdown for dashboards / assertions |
| `sweep_expired` | `() -> list[str]` | ids of reclaimed holds | — | Background sweep releases timed-out holds |

> 🎙️ "The whole system is seven calls — one to stock, three that drive the two-phase flow (`reserve` → `confirm`/`release`), two read paths, and one background sweep. If you handed me just this table, you'd already know the entire lifecycle of a unit of stock without seeing a single class — and you'd notice `reserve` is the only one that both reads and mutates a shared count, which is exactly where the race lives."

---

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### `InventoryService` — the orchestrator

**(a) Role** — The system's public API and single source of truth. Every reserve/confirm/release flows through here; it owns the registries and coordinates the entities but never duplicates their logic.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "Add stock for a SKU / query available" | A map: sku_id → `StockItem` |
| "Confirm/release/sweep reference a hold" | A map: reservation_id → `Reservation` |
| "Holds optionally expire by a policy" | The injected `ReservationExpiryPolicy` |
| "Test expiry without sleeping" | An injected clock function `now` |
| "Issue readable, unique hold ids" | An id counter |

*State placement trade-off:* the available/reserved/sold counts are **physical state of the SKU** — they describe the SKU's own condition — so they live on `StockItem`, not the service. The service only holds the **relational** state: which SKUs and holds exist and how they're indexed. That's the Information-Expert split.

```
class InventoryService:
    - now: Callable[[], float]
    - policy: ReservationExpiryPolicy
    - stock: Map<str, StockItem>
    - reservations: Map<str, Reservation>
    - ids: counter
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "Add stock for a SKU" | `add_stock(sku, quantity) -> None` |
| "Reserve units (phase 1)" | `reserve(sku_id, quantity) -> str` |
| "Confirm a reservation (phase 2a)" | `confirm(reservation_id) -> None` |
| "Release a reservation (phase 2b)" | `release(reservation_id) -> None` |
| "Query free units / full breakdown" | `available(sku_id) -> int`, `snapshot(sku_id) -> StockSnapshot` |
| "Expire stale holds" | `sweep_expired() -> list[str]` |
| "Concurrency seam (swap-in lock)" | `_lock(sku_id)` (no-op hook) |

```
class InventoryService:
    - now: Callable[[], float]
    - policy: ReservationExpiryPolicy
    - stock: Map<str, StockItem>
    - reservations: Map<str, Reservation>
    - ids: counter

    + InventoryService(expiry_policy, now_fn)
    + add_stock(sku, quantity) -> None
    + reserve(sku_id, quantity) -> str | error
    + confirm(reservation_id) -> None | error
    + release(reservation_id) -> None | error
    + available(sku_id) -> int | error
    + snapshot(sku_id) -> StockSnapshot | error

    # _lock(sku_id)                      # no-op hook; subclass overrides

    # _next_id() -> str
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `reserve` return a bare string id, not the `Reservation`?** The caller only needs a handle to confirm/release later; handing back the live object would let them mutate my state. The id is an opaque token, like a claim check.
- **Why inject `now_fn` and the policy?** So expiry is testable without sleeping and the timeout rule is swappable. The service never calls `time.monotonic()` directly inside its logic.
- **Why two maps instead of one?** Holds and stock have different lifetimes and lookup keys — `confirm` looks up by hold id, `available` by SKU id. One map keyed by SKU couldn't answer "confirm this hold" in O(1).

#### `StockItem` — the count machine (Information Expert for the invariant)

**(a) Role** — Owns one SKU's three buckets and is the only place units move between them. The conservation invariant is true *by construction* here.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "available / reserved / sold buckets" | three int counts |
| "which SKU these counts belong to" | `sku_id` |

```
class StockItem:
    - sku_id: str
    - available: int
    - reserved: int
    - sold: int
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "Reserve moves available → reserved (oversell guard)" | `reserve(quantity) -> None` |
| "Confirm moves reserved → sold" | `confirm(quantity) -> None` |
| "Release moves reserved → available" | `release(quantity) -> None` |
| "Restock grows available" | `add(quantity) -> None` |
| "Conservation total / read view" | `total -> int`, `snapshot() -> StockSnapshot` |

```
class StockItem:
    - sku_id: str
    - available: int
    - reserved: int
    - sold: int

    + reserve(quantity) -> None | InsufficientStockError
    + confirm(quantity) -> None | InvalidReservationStateError
    + release(quantity) -> None | InvalidReservationStateError
    + add(quantity) -> None
    + total() -> int
    + snapshot() -> StockSnapshot
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why guard `reserve` before mutating?** A rejected reserve must leave all three buckets untouched — check-then-act in one method body keeps "saw available" and "decremented available" together.
- **Why does the entity raise, not the service?** The entity is the expert on its own counts; centralizing the bucket math means no caller can ever bypass the underflow checks.

#### `Reservation` — the hold lifecycle (state machine)

**(a) Role** — One hold moving through HELD → CONFIRMED / RELEASED / EXPIRED. It owns the lifecycle so illegal transitions are impossible; the service never writes `status` directly.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "a hold id the caller gets back" | `id` |
| "which SKU and how many units" | `sku_id`, `quantity` |
| "where it is in its life" | `status: ReservationStatus` |
| "when it expires (optional)" | `expires_at: float \| None`, `created_at: float` |

```
class Reservation:
    - id: str
    - sku_id: str
    - quantity: int
    - status: ReservationStatus
    - created_at: float
    - expires_at: float | None
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "Confirm once; reject second confirm" | `confirm() -> None` |
| "Release idempotently; reject release of a sale" | `release() -> bool` |
| "Expire a still-held hold" | `expire() -> bool` |
| "Has this hold's timer elapsed?" | `is_expired(now) -> bool` |

```
class Reservation:
    - id: str
    - sku_id: str
    - quantity: int
    - status: ReservationStatus
    - created_at: float
    - expires_at: float | None

    + confirm() -> None | InvalidReservationStateError
    + release() -> bool                       # False if already terminal (no-op)
    + expire() -> bool                        # False if not HELD
    + is_expired(now) -> bool
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why does `release` return a bool while `confirm` returns None?** `release` tells the service "did I actually transition?" so the service knows whether to hand units back — that's how double-release stays a clean no-op. `confirm` is strict: a second call raises.
- **Why keep `created_at` AND `expires_at`?** `expires_at` is computed once from the policy at creation; keeping `created_at` makes the hold self-describing for logging and future relative-expiry policies.

#### `ReservationExpiryPolicy` — the Strategy seam (interface)

**(a) Role** — The abstraction the service depends on to answer "for a hold created at time T, when does it expire?" The service never hardcodes a timeout.

**(b) State** — none (pure behavior). Concretes may carry config (e.g. a TTL).

```
interface ReservationExpiryPolicy:
    expires_at(created_at: float) -> float | None
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
|---|---|
| "Decide when a hold expires (or never)" | `expires_at(created_at) -> float \| None` |

```
interface ReservationExpiryPolicy:
    + expires_at(created_at: float) -> float | None

class NoExpiryPolicy(ReservationExpiryPolicy):
    + expires_at(created_at) -> None          # never times out

class FixedTtlExpiryPolicy(ReservationExpiryPolicy):
    - ttl: float
    + FixedTtlExpiryPolicy(ttl_seconds)
    + expires_at(created_at) -> created_at + ttl
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why return an absolute instant, not a duration?** The `Reservation` stores one number and `is_expired(now)` is a single comparison — no re-deriving the deadline on every check.
- **Why is `None` a first-class return?** It models "never expires" without a separate flag, so the sweep simply skips holds whose `expires_at` is `None`.

#### `Sku` and `StockSnapshot` — frozen value objects

**(a) Role** — `Sku` is the stable product identity; `StockSnapshot` is a read-only count view handed to callers. Both frozen so nothing downstream can mutate them.

**(b)/(c)** — pure data, no behavior beyond a derived `total`:

```
@frozen class Sku:
    - sku_id: str
    - name: str

@frozen class StockSnapshot:
    - sku_id: str
    - available: int
    - reserved: int
    - sold: int
    + total() -> int                          # available + reserved + sold
```

**(d) Design choice** — **Why hand back a `StockSnapshot` instead of the live `StockItem`?** A caller reading "5 left" must not be able to reach in and change my reserved count. The frozen snapshot is a safe copy.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)

I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to its data. `StockItem` owns the available/reserved/sold counts and the rules that move units between them, because it's the only thing that sees all three buckets — so the conservation invariant is enforced where the data lives, not scattered across the service. `Reservation` owns its own status transitions because the lifecycle rule (confirm-once, release-idempotent) is intrinsic to a hold. `InventoryService` owns only the *relational* state — which SKUs and holds exist, and how they're indexed — because it's the one thing that sees the whole system. That split is why the orchestrator stays thin: it coordinates, it doesn't compute.

### 5.2 Applied patterns (each one earned)

- **Two-Phase Reserve/Commit** — *Why it fits HERE:* the whole point of this problem is that "held during payment" and "sold" are different states, and a held unit must be undoable. So `reserve` sets stock aside and returns a handle, then `confirm` commits it to sold or `release` rolls it back. **Seam:** `reserve() → confirm()/release()` on `InventoryService`, with `StockItem` moving units between buckets. 🎙️ "Reserve is the 'tentative' step, confirm is the 'commit', release is the 'undo' — exactly like a two-phase commit but at the stock level." 💬 *Layman:* "It's like putting an item on hold at a shop counter — it's set aside for you, but it's not yours until you actually pay; if you walk away, it goes back on the shelf."

- **State (state machine)** — *Why it fits HERE:* a hold has a small, ordered set of legal moves and the failure mode (confirm twice, release a sale) is exactly an illegal jump. **Seam:** `Reservation.confirm/release/expire` flip `status` and reject illegal transitions. 🎙️ "Only the `Reservation` flips its own status, so a double-confirm is impossible — the second call hits the HELD guard and raises." 💬 *Layman:* "The hold remembers what's already happened to it, so it won't let you sell the same thing twice."

- **Strategy** — *Why it fits HERE:* the expiry rule is exactly the thing the business will want to tweak — 15-minute carts today, 90-second flash sales tomorrow. **Seam:** `ReservationExpiryPolicy.expires_at` with `FixedTtlExpiryPolicy` / `NoExpiryPolicy`. 🎙️ "The service asks a policy when a hold expires; swapping the rule is a new class, not a service edit." 💬 *Layman:* "The system doesn't hardcode 'holds last 15 minutes' — it asks a rule-of-the-day, so we can change the timeout without rewiring anything."

### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific)

- **Single Responsibility:** `StockItem` owns counts, `Reservation` owns lifecycle, `ReservationExpiryPolicy` owns the timeout rule, `InventoryService` owns coordination. Four reasons to change, four classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new timeout rule = a new `ReservationExpiryPolicy` subclass, zero core edits. Thread-safety = a new subclass overriding `_lock`, zero core edits.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `InventoryService` depends on the `ReservationExpiryPolicy` abstraction, never on `FixedTtlExpiryPolicy` directly — it's injected in the constructor.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeInventoryService` is a drop-in for `InventoryService` — same public surface, only `_lock` differs, so every test that passes on the core passes on the subclass.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS

Every claim above is `Cmd-F`-able in §6: the Strategy interface is a real ABC with two real implementations injected into the constructor; the State machine is real `status` guards on `Reservation`; the Template Method `_lock` is a real `@contextmanager` overridden in a real subclass. I did **not** claim Singleton (there's no enforced single instance), Factory (no enum→object translation — construction is plain), or Observer (no notification seam in v1; I note where I'd add one in §9). Those would be name-dropping.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

> **Insight:** Split the two concerns that look like one — make `StockItem` own the COUNTS (and the conservation invariant) and make `Reservation` own the LIFECYCLE (and the legal-transition rule). The orchestrator just sequences them under a per-SKU lock.
>
> **Why it works:** A unit of stock is always in exactly one of three buckets, and every operation is a *move* between buckets — so if the only code that touches the counts is `StockItem`'s four methods, the total is conserved automatically. Independently, a hold has a tiny finite state machine, so if only `Reservation` flips its status, illegal transitions can't happen. Neither concern needs to know about the other.
>
> **What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines `item.available -= qty; item.reserved += qty` and `res.status = CONFIRMED` directly in the service. Six months later someone adds a "partial confirm" path and forgets the underflow guard in one spot — now `reserved` goes negative and a refund double-restores stock, an oversell that's almost impossible to trace. By making the entities the experts, that bug is impossible by construction: there's exactly one place units move and one place status flips.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm

**`reserve(sku_id, quantity)` — the pipeline (race-prone)**

(1) Pseudocode:
```
1. validate quantity > 0                      else InvalidQuantityError
2. look up StockItem for sku_id               else SkuNotFoundError
3. ── enter per-SKU lock ──
4.   if quantity > item.available: raise InsufficientStockError   (oversell guard)
5.   item.available -= quantity; item.reserved += quantity
6.   stamp expires_at = policy.expires_at(now())
7.   build Reservation(id, sku_id, qty, HELD, expires_at)
8.   reservations[id] = reservation
9. ── exit lock ──
10. return id
```

(2) 🎙️ **This method has the check-then-act race.** "Steps 4-5 are the critical section — the check 'is there enough?' and the act 'take it' must be one atomic step, or two carts both pass step 4 on the last unit. Step 4 is the oversell guard; step 6 is where the Strategy fires — `policy.expires_at` decides the timeout without the service knowing the rule. I'll wrap steps 4-8 in a per-SKU lock in §9."

(3) **DRY RUN — `reserve("FLASH", 1)` with `StockItem(available=5, reserved=0, sold=0)`:**

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | validate qty 1 > 0 | ✓ |
| 2 | look up StockItem("FLASH") | found ✓ |
| 4 | 1 > 5? no | guard passes |
| 5 | move 1 available→reserved | available 5→4, reserved 0→1 |
| 6 | `policy.expires_at(now)` | NoExpiry → `None` |
| 7-8 | build + index hold | `reservations["res-1"]=HELD(qty=1)` |
| 10 | return id | caller gets `"res-1"` |

> *Edge probe: a second cart calls `reserve("FLASH", 1)` while step 5 is mid-flight?* Without the lock both read available=5 at step 4 and both decrement → available=3, reserved=2 for what should be 1 sale of the last unit story — that's the oversell. §9 fixes it: the per-SKU lock makes steps 4-8 atomic, so the second cart waits, then re-checks at step 4 and sees the true remaining count.

**`confirm(reservation_id)` — the commit**

(1) Pseudocode:
```
1. look up Reservation by id                  else ReservationNotFoundError
2. ── enter per-SKU lock (res.sku_id) ──
3.   if HELD and expired: expire it + release stock, then proceed to step 4 (will reject)
4.   reservation.confirm()                    rejects non-HELD (double-confirm / expired)
5.   item.confirm(reservation.quantity)       reserved -= qty; sold += qty
6. ── exit lock ──
```

(2) 🎙️ **Confirm is one guard and one commit.** "Step 3 lazily reclaims an expired hold so a confirm arriving a millisecond after expiry fails cleanly instead of selling stock that was already given back. Step 4 is where the State machine fires — `reservation.confirm()` rejects a second confirm because the hold is no longer HELD."

**`release(reservation_id)` — the undo (idempotent)**

(1) Pseudocode:
```
1. look up Reservation by id                  else ReservationNotFoundError
2. ── enter per-SKU lock ──
3.   if HELD and expired: expire+release, return (already restored)
4.   if reservation.release() is True:        False => already terminal (no-op)
5.       item.release(reservation.quantity)   reserved -= qty; available += qty
6. ── exit lock ──
```

(2) 🎙️ **Release is idempotent on purpose.** "Step 4's bool is the trick — if the hold is already RELEASED/EXPIRED, `release()` returns False and we skip the stock move, so a user-cancel racing the sweep can't double-restore. A CONFIRMED hold raises instead — a sale can't be silently un-sold."

**`sweep_expired()` — the background cleanup**

(1) Pseudocode:
```
1. now = clock()
2. for each reservation that is HELD and is_expired(now):
3.    ── enter per-SKU lock ──
4.      expire it + release its stock; record id
5.    ── exit lock ──
6. return reclaimed ids
```

(2) 🎙️ **This runs in the background. Idempotent on purpose** — "each iteration is independent and a hold that's already terminal is skipped, so calling sweep twice reclaims nothing the second time. A timer thread calls this every minute in production."

**`add_stock` / `available` / `snapshot`** — `add_stock` registers-or-restocks under the SKU lock; `available` and `snapshot` are O(1) reads of the `StockItem` under the lock. (Pseudocode is a single dict lookup each — covered in §6.)

### C. Data Structure Choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[str, StockItem]` | SKU → counts registry | `available`/`reserve` are O(1) by SKU id; a list would be O(n) per lookup on the hot path | O(1) get/set |
| `dict[str, Reservation]` | hold id → hold | `confirm`/`release` look up by hold id; scanning a list of live holds on every confirm would be O(n) → O(1) | O(1) get/set, O(n) sweep |
| `dict[str, RLock]` (subclass) | per-SKU lock striping | one lock per SKU so different SKUs run in parallel; a single global lock would serialize unrelated products | O(1) lock fetch |
| three `int` counts on `StockItem` | available/reserved/sold | direct bucket math keeps the invariant trivial; a list of per-unit objects would be O(n) memory for zero benefit | O(1) per move |
| `itertools.count` | hold id generation | `__next__` is atomic in CPython, so unique ids across SKUs need no extra lock; a guarded counter adds contention | O(1) |

The `sweep_expired` scan is O(n) over all reservations. I'd flip to a min-heap or a time-bucketed index keyed by `expires_at` when active holds cross ~10K and the sweep becomes a measurable hot spot — until then the linear scan every minute is free.

### D. Tricky Logic

> **🧠 Claim:** `reserve`'s availability check and decrement must be one atomic step.
> **🔬 Proof:** Two carts call `reserve("FLASH", 1)` on the last unit. Both run step 4 (`1 > 5`? no), both run step 5, available goes 5→4→3 for what should be one sale — two holds exist for stock that's now mis-counted. Oversell.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** steps 4-8 run inside `with self._lock(sku_id)`; the thread-safe subclass makes `_lock` a per-SKU `RLock`. The 64-thread Barrier test in §7/§9 proves exactly K succeed.

> **🧠 Claim:** `release` must be idempotent; `confirm` must not be.
> **🔬 Proof:** A user clicks "cancel" the same instant the expiry sweep fires. Both call `release` on the same HELD hold. If release weren't idempotent, the second call would move `reserved → available` twice and over-restore stock.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Reservation.release()` returns `False` if already RELEASED/EXPIRED, and the service only moves stock when it returns `True`. `confirm()` instead raises on a non-HELD hold (double-confirm rejected).

> **🧠 Claim:** A confirm arriving just after a hold expires must NOT sell stock.
> **🔬 Proof:** Hold expires at t=60; the sweep reclaims its units back to available at t=61; a late `confirm` at t=61.5 would otherwise move already-returned units to sold — double-counting.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `confirm` calls `_expire_if_due` first; the expired hold is now EXPIRED, so `reservation.confirm()` hits the HELD guard and raises `InvalidReservationStateError`.

> **🧠 Claim:** Per-SKU locking, not one global lock, is required for throughput.
> **🔬 Proof:** With one global lock, a flash sale on SKU-A serializes every reserve on the entire catalog — SKU-B checkouts block on a lock they don't need.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ThreadSafeInventoryService` keeps `dict[sku_id, RLock]`; only same-SKU calls serialize. The dict itself is guarded by a separate `_map_lock` so concurrent first-touches of two SKUs can't corrupt it.

> **🧠 Claim:** Injected clock is necessary for fast, deterministic expiry tests.
> **🔬 Proof:** Testing a 60-second TTL by `time.sleep(61)` makes the suite slow and flaky.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `now_fn` is injected; tests pass `lambda: clock["t"]` and jump the clock forward — expiry tests run in microseconds.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def reserve(self, sku_id, quantity):
>     item = self._stock[sku_id]
>     if item.available >= quantity:        # bare check
>         item.available -= quantity         # direct write, separate from check
>         item.reserved += quantity
>         return self._make_reservation(sku_id, quantity)
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** Three failure modes. (1) The check and the two writes are check-then-act — under concurrency two callers both pass the `if` and both decrement → oversell. (2) Direct count writes bypass `StockItem`'s underflow guards, so a future bug can drive `reserved` negative. (3) The success-only `if` silently returns `None` when stock is short instead of raising — the caller can't tell "reserved nothing" from "reserved something". The correct version delegates to `item.reserve()` (one guarded atomic move that raises), and lifts the whole block under a per-SKU lock in §9.
